text: The surgeon places the first two laparoscopic ports and performs an extensive lysis of adhesions within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon identifies the falciform ligament, a thin fold of peritoneum visible in the upper abdominal cavity.

The surgeon maneuvers instruments to create space for the placement of the third and fourth robotic trocars in the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon operates in the hypogastrium region, manipulating tissues and structures visible in this area.

The surgeon exposes the midline, revealing the underlying anatomical structures in preparation for further surgical steps.
The surgeon exposes the midline, revealing previously placed proline sutures visible along the incision line.
The previously placed proline sutures are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon has created space for the placement of the third robotic arm in the surgical field.
The surgeon has created space for the third robotic arm and continues the lysis of adhesions in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to lace and separate adhesions in the surgical field, focusing on the hypogastrium region.
The surgeon manipulates tissue to create space for the insertion of the fourth robotic arm in the hypogastrium region.
The surgeon manipulates tissues to create space for the placement of the fourth robotic arm in the surgical field.

The surgeon has successfully created space in the surgical field to accommodate the fourth robotic arm, visible alongside the third arm and previously placed proline sutures in the midline.
A 12mm trocar is placed in the left upper quadrant at the original trocar position, followed by the commencement of robotic lysis of adhesions to fully expose the abdominal cavity.
A twelve millimeter trocar is placed in the left upper quadrant at the original trocar position, followed by the commencement of robotic lysis of adhesions to fully expose the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon exposes the midline, revealing a 'Swiss cheese' appearance characterized by multiple openings or defects in the tissue.
The surgeon exteriorizes the closure through a right upper quadrant trocar site, avoiding the midline incision area.
The surgeon avoids the midline for closure by exteriorizing through a trocar site in the right upper quadrant.
The surgeon uses a zero V-Loc suture to placate and close the visible Swiss cheese hernia defects along the midline.
The surgeon placates the midline upon closure using a zero V lock suture to close the visible Swiss cheese hernia defects without employing mesh.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to enter the gastrohepatic ligament, exposing the transverse colon tumor within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to enter the gastrohepatic ligament, exposing the transverse colon tumor.
The surgeon navigates above the transverse colon into the lesser sac, continuing the dissection in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon navigates above the transverse colon into the lesser sac, preparing to expose the area for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon continues to detach the omentum from the transverse colon, aiming to fully expose the tumor located in this region.
The surgeon continues to dissect the omentum from the transverse colon, aiming to fully expose the tumor located in this region.
The surgeon observes residual omentum adherent in the pelvic region that has not been freed during the operation.
The surgeon amputates the adherent omentum in the pelvis, with plans to resect the remaining dead omentum later in the procedure.
The surgeon amputates the transverse colon tumor using surgical instruments, following exposure through the gastrohepatic ligament.

The surgeon has exposed the tumor and is performing a mesenteric dissection by closely following the bowel wall, avoiding an oncologic resection due to the patient's comorbidities and compromised blood supply to the colon.

The surgeon has exposed the tumor on the transverse colon and is performing a mesenteric dissection by closely following the bowel wall. The tumor appears obstructive, and a palliative resection is being conducted due to the patient's medical comorbidities and previous surgical history.
The surgeon carefully dissects underneath the bowel wall, maintaining close proximity to the tissue during the mesenteric dissection.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to carefully dissect underneath the bowel wall, maintaining close proximity to the tissue.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to effectively seal blood vessels during the procedure.
The surgeon creates a window through the mesentery, continuing to dissect and separate the remaining mesenteric tissue from the specimen.

The surgeon administers indocyanine green (ICG) dye to visually confirm the integrity of the blood supply to the colon, ensuring proper perfusion following the resection.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) dye to assess the blood supply to the colon, visually confirming that the vascularization appears intact.
The surgeon inserts a trocar into the right upper quadrant and proceeds to divide the tissue proximally, focusing on the area near the bowel wall.
The surgeon inserts a trocar into the right upper quadrant and proceeds to divide tissue proximally and then distally.
The surgeon aligns an isoparastaltic anastomosis, creates enterotomies using scissors, and uses a white load stapler in the right upper quadrant to form a common channel.
The surgeon aligns the bowel segments for an isoparastaltic anastomosis, makes enterotomies with scissors, and uses a white load stapler in the right upper quadrant to create a common channel.
The surgeon closes the common enterotomy, ensuring the continuity of the intestinal tract following the anastomosis formation.
The surgeon closes the common enterotomy using a suture, completing the second layer of closure.
The surgeon completes the second layer of suturing to close the enterotomy, using the same suture material as previously applied.
The surgeon covers the surgical site with omentum, ensuring the area is protected and supported by the fatty tissue layer.
The surgeon covers the surgical site with omentum, having resected the necrotic omentum tissue. The midline is then placated using a zero V-lock suture.
The surgeon resects the necrotic omentum and uses a zero V-lock suture to placate the midline.
